section.title: Choose Life
section.sectionRationale: Looking beyond the curses of exile just described, Moses promises restoration when Israel returns to the LORD, culminating in the astonishing promise that God Himself will circumcise Israel's heart, and closes with the covenant's climactic appeal: the command is not too difficult, and Israel must choose life.
section.thesis: Beyond exile and curse, the LORD promises restoration for a returning people, capped by the astonishing pledge that He Himself will circumcise their hearts to love Him; because His word is near, in the mouth and the heart, not impossibly far away, Moses closes the whole covenant with heaven and earth as witnesses: choose life.
section.frameNote: Four movements: restoration promised when Israel returns to the LORD from exile; the LORD's own promise to circumcise Israel's heart so they can truly love Him; the nearness of the command, not in heaven or beyond the sea, but in the mouth and the heart; and the climactic appeal, with heaven and earth as witnesses, to choose life.

units.label: Restoration after exile
units.note: when you return to the LORD

verses.text: "When all these things come upon you, the blessings and curses I have set before you, and you call them to mind in all the nations to which the LORD your God has banished you,
and when you and your children {{shuv|return}} to the LORD your God and obey His voice with all your heart and all your soul according to everything I am giving you today,
then He will restore you from captivity and have compassion on you and gather you from all the nations to which the LORD your God has scattered you.
Even if you have been banished to the farthest horizon, He will gather you and return you from there.
And the LORD your God will bring you into the land your fathers possessed, and you will take possession of it. He will cause you to prosper and multiply more than your fathers.

units.label: The LORD will circumcise your heart

verses.text: The LORD your God will {{mool|circumcise}} your hearts and the hearts of your descendants, and you will love Him with all your heart and with all your soul, so that you may live.
Then the LORD your God will put all these curses upon your enemies who hate you and persecute you.
And you will again obey the voice of the LORD and follow all His commandments I am giving you today.

So the LORD your God will make you abound in all the work of your hands and in the fruit of your womb, the offspring of your livestock, and the produce of your land. Indeed, the LORD will again delight in your goodness, as He delighted in that of your fathers,
if you obey the LORD your God by keeping His commandments and statutes that are written in this Book of the Law, and if you turn to Him with all your heart and with all your soul.

units.label: The word is very near you
units.note: not too difficult, not far away

verses.text: For this commandment I give you today is not too difficult for you or beyond your reach.
It is not in heaven, that you should need to ask, 'Who will ascend into heaven to get it for us and proclaim it, that we may obey it?'
And it is not beyond the sea, that you should need to ask, 'Who will cross the sea to get it for us and proclaim it, that we may obey it?'
But the word is very near you; it is in your mouth and in your heart, so that you may obey it.
See, I have set before you today life and goodness, as well as death and disaster.

For I am commanding you today to love the LORD your God, to walk in His ways, and to keep His commandments, statutes, and ordinances, so that you may live and increase, and the LORD your God may bless you in the land that you are entering to possess.
But if your heart turns away and you do not listen, but are drawn away to bow down to other gods and worship them,
I declare to you today that you will surely perish; you shall not prolong your days in the land that you are crossing the Jordan to possess.
I call heaven and earth as witnesses against you today that I have set before you life and death, blessing and cursing. Therefore {{bachar|choose}} life, so that you and your descendants may live,

and that you may love the LORD your God, obey Him, and hold fast to Him. For He is your life, and He will prolong your life in the land that the LORD swore to give to your fathers, to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob."

shuv.gloss: To return, to turn back, to repent. Israel's restoration begins when they 'return' to the LORD, the same root behind much of the Old Testament's vocabulary for repentance.

mool.gloss: To circumcise. Here applied not to the flesh but to the heart: 'the LORD your God will circumcise your heart,' promising an inward transformation beyond what the sign of the old covenant could accomplish, fulfilled in the Spirit's work under the new covenant, Colossians 2:11.

bachar.gloss: To choose. Moses' climactic command, 'choose life,' holds together God's sovereign initiative in circumcising the heart, verse 6, and Israel's genuine responsibility to choose covenant faithfulness, verse 19, a tension held, not erased, in the gospel.

basic.context: Having just heard the full weight of covenant curses, including exile, Israel now hears a promise beyond judgment: when they return to the LORD with all their heart, He will restore them, gather them from wherever they are scattered, and do something the law itself could never do, circumcise their hearts so they can truly love Him. Moses insists God's command is not impossibly distant, it is near, in their mouth and heart, and closes the entire covenant with a solemn choice: life and blessing, or death and curse. Choose life.
basic.hermeneutics: This is Israel's real, historical hope of restoration after real, historical exile, later partially fulfilled in the return from Babylon; yet the promise to 'circumcise your heart' goes beyond mere geography, describing an inward transformation the old covenant law could command but never produce, fulfilled fully in the new covenant Christ inaugurates. 'Choose life' holds together real human responsibility and God's prior, sovereign work of grace without collapsing either into the other.
basic.originalLanguages: Israel's restoration begins when they shuv, return or repent, to the LORD. The astonishing promise is that God will mool, circumcise, their hearts, an inward transformation beyond the outward sign of the old covenant. And Moses' climactic command is to bachar, choose, life, holding together God's sovereign initiative and genuine human responsibility.
basic.typology: The circumcised heart promised here anticipates the new covenant Jeremiah and Ezekiel describe, a law written on the heart and a new spirit given, fulfilled when Christ's Spirit indwells believers and produces from within what the old covenant's external law could only demand from without.
basic.christ: Paul quotes Deuteronomy 30:11-14 directly in Romans 10:6-8, applying 'the word is near you, in your mouth and in your heart' to the gospel of Christ: righteousness is not achieved by ascending to heaven or descending to the depths, but received by the word of faith already near in the good news of Jesus, crucified and risen.
basic.god: God does not merely command from a distance and leave His people to fail; He promises to do in them what they could never do for themselves, circumcising the heart itself, showing that even under the old covenant, grace, not bare human effort, was always the true ground of hope.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 10:6-8 quotes this passage directly to explain the nearness of the gospel. Jeremiah 31:31-33 promises the new covenant law written on the heart. Ezekiel 36:26-27 promises a new heart and God's own Spirit within His people. Colossians 2:11 describes believers as having 'a circumcision not performed by human hands,' the circumcision of Christ.
basic.oneStory: This chapter's promise, that God Himself will circumcise the heart, exposes the deepest need the law could never meet and the deepest hope the whole Bible moves toward: a people who do not merely hear commands from outside but are changed within, made possible fully in Christ and given by His Spirit.

context.literary: This chapter is the theological hinge of the whole covenant renewal section, chapters 27-30, moving from curse and exile to the hope of restoration and finally to the covenant's climactic appeal to choose.
context.historical: Israel's later history bears out both halves of this chapter: real exile under Assyria and Babylon, and a real, partial return under Ezra and Nehemiah, though the fuller heart-transformation this chapter promises awaits the new covenant.
context.canonical: Paul's direct use of verses 11-14 in Romans 10 makes this one of the clearest Old Testament texts showing continuity between Moses' preaching and the gospel of Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To hold before Israel both the real hope of restoration after exile and the deeper need for a transformed heart that only God can give, closing with a clear, urgent call to choose covenant faithfulness.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific historical promise of restoration to the land describes Israel's particular covenant history; the promise of a circumcised heart points forward typologically to the new covenant fulfilled in Christ, and the call to 'choose life' remains a directly prescriptive, ongoing appeal to every hearer of God's word in every generation.

debates.question: How do God's sovereign initiative, 'the LORD will circumcise your heart,' verse 6, and Israel's responsibility, 'choose life,' verse 19, fit together in this chapter?

views.label: God's grace precedes and enables the human choice
views.case: Verse 6 describes God's own prior, gracious act on the heart, without which Israel could not truly choose life at all; the call to choose in verse 19 assumes and depends on the transforming work God promises to do first.

views.label: Human responsibility remains genuine and is not diminished by grace
views.case: Moses still directly commands, pleads, and holds Israel accountable for the choice in verses 15-20; the text does not treat human responsibility as an illusion even while affirming God's decisive, prior grace.

debates.bearing: Both are affirmed together in the text without resolution into either pure human free will or a fatalism that removes responsibility; Scripture consistently holds divine sovereignty and genuine human responsibility together, as it does here.

study.originalLanguages: Israel's restoration begins when they shuv, return, verse 2, to the LORD. God will mool, circumcise, verse 6, their hearts. And Moses' climactic command is to bachar, choose, verse 19, life, holding together God's sovereign initiative and genuine human responsibility.

typology.fulfillment: The new covenant's law written on the heart and new spirit given, Jeremiah 31:33, Ezekiel 36:26-27, fulfilled through Christ and His Spirit.

typology.type: The word near in mouth and heart
typology.fulfillment: Paul applies this directly to the nearness of gospel faith in Christ, Romans 10:6-8.

study.christ: Paul quotes Deuteronomy 30:11-14 directly in Romans 10:6-8, applying 'the word is near you, in your mouth and in your heart' to the gospel of Christ: righteousness is not achieved by ascending to heaven or descending to the depths, but received by the word of faith already near in the good news of Jesus, crucified and risen.
study.god: God does not merely command from a distance and leave His people to fail; He promises to do in them what they could never do for themselves, circumcising the heart itself, showing that even under the old covenant, grace, not bare human effort, was always the true ground of hope.

crossReferences.note: Quotes this passage directly to explain the nearness of the gospel.
Promises the new covenant law written on the heart.
Promises a new heart and God's own Spirit within His people.
Describes believers as having 'a circumcision not performed by human hands,' the circumcision of Christ.

study.oneStory: This chapter's promise, that God Himself will circumcise the heart, exposes the deepest need the law could never meet and the deepest hope the whole Bible moves toward: a people who do not merely hear commands from outside but are changed within, made possible fully in Christ and given by His Spirit.

sermon.title: An Impossible Covenant, And How It Becomes a Permanent Joy
sermon.why: The second half of Piper's message moves directly into Deuteronomy 30:6-20, culminating in 'choose life,' explaining how God's circumcision of the heart and Christ's cross make genuine obedience possible, a direct match for this study's passage and thesis.

quiz.q: What does the LORD promise to do for Israel's heart in verse 6?

options.text: Circumcise it, so that Israel will love the LORD with all their heart and soul.
options.note: Verse 6 states this explicit promise.

options.text: Harden it permanently against repentance.
options.note: The text promises the opposite, an enabling of love and repentance.

options.text: Replace it with a heart of stone.
options.note: The text promises the removal of hindrance to love, not hardening.

options.text: No promise is made about Israel's heart.
options.note: Verse 6 gives an explicit promise.

quiz.q: According to verses 11-14, where is the LORD's commandment located?

options.text: It is not in heaven or beyond the sea; it is very near, in your mouth and in your heart.
options.note: Verses 11-14 state this explicitly.

options.text: It is locked away in heaven, inaccessible to anyone.
options.note: The text explicitly denies this, insisting the word is near.

options.text: It is hidden across the sea, requiring a difficult journey to obtain.
options.note: The text explicitly denies this as well.

options.text: Its location is never addressed.
options.note: Verses 11-14 address this directly and explicitly.

quiz.q: How does Paul use Deuteronomy 30:11-14 in Romans 10:6-8?

options.text: He applies it to the nearness of the gospel, righteousness by faith in the crucified and risen Christ, not by ascending to heaven or descending to the depths.
options.note: Romans 10:6-8 makes this application explicit.

options.text: He argues the passage is entirely irrelevant to the gospel.
options.note: Paul treats it as directly, positively relevant to explaining the gospel's nearness.

options.text: He applies it only to the Ten Commandments.
options.note: Paul specifically applies it to gospel faith in Christ, not the Decalogue.

options.text: Paul never references this passage.
options.note: Romans 10:6-8 is an explicit, direct application.

quiz.q: What two options does Moses set before Israel in verse 15?

options.text: Life and goodness, as well as death and disaster.
options.note: Verse 15 states this explicit pairing.

options.text: Wealth and poverty only.
options.note: The stated options are life and death, not merely wealth and poverty.

options.text: War and peace only.
options.note: The stated options are life and death, a broader and more fundamental choice.

options.text: No options are given.
options.note: Verse 15 gives explicit options.

quiz.q: What does Moses call as witnesses to the choice set before Israel in verse 19?

options.text: Heaven and earth.
options.note: Verse 19 states this explicitly.

options.text: The surrounding nations.
options.note: The text specifically names heaven and earth, not neighboring nations.

options.text: The tribe of Levi alone.
options.note: The text names heaven and earth as witnesses, not a single tribe.

options.text: No witnesses are named.
options.note: Verse 19 explicitly names heaven and earth as witnesses.

questions: God promised to circumcise Israel's heart, doing what the law alone could never do. Where in your life have you been relying on willpower alone rather than trusting God to change your heart from within?
Moses insisted God's command was not impossibly distant but near, in the mouth and the heart. Where do you treat obedience to God as too difficult or out of reach, when His word says otherwise?

Paul takes this very passage and applies it to the nearness of the gospel in Christ. How near or distant does the good news of Jesus feel to you right now, and what would it look like to receive it as near?
Moses set life and death plainly before Israel and called heaven and earth as witnesses to their choice. What choice are you avoiding making plain and deciding for yourself today?
'Choose life' assumes both God's prior grace and Israel's real responsibility to choose. How do you hold together trusting God's work in you and still taking real, active steps of obedience?

translationNotes.note: 'Circumcise your heart' uses the sign of the old covenant, physical circumcision, as a metaphor for an inward, spiritual transformation; compare Deuteronomy 10:16, where Israel is commanded to circumcise their own hearts, here it becomes something God Himself promises to do for them.

Paul quotes this passage nearly word for word in Romans 10:6-8, reapplying 'the word is near you' from the law of Moses to the gospel of Christ, showing early Christian confidence that the Old Testament itself anticipated the good news.
Calling 'heaven and earth' as witnesses reflects ancient Near Eastern treaty language, invoking the created order itself as an enduring witness to the terms of the covenant being agreed to.
